claim: Hand A text follows medieval pharmaceutical recipe structure: paragraph-initial ingredient/topic names (LOW-class, rarely reused in running text), paragraph-medial preparation instructions (HIGH-class verbs, reused across all folios), and boundary-terminal ingredients or dosage. The H-BV-HIGH-LOW-STRUCTURE-01 finding that paragraph-initial tokens are 87.5% LOW-class and that HIGH/LOW classes anti-alternate in runs is consistent with this recipe structure; this test directly verifies it.
evidence_for: H-BV-HIGH-LOW-STRUCTURE-01 found paragraph-initial tokens are 87.5% LOW-class, HIGH/LOW classes anti-alternate (cross-class 46.9% vs 50% random), and clusters form runs rather than interleaving — directly consistent with recipe structure

H-BV-HAND-A-INTERNAL-01 confirmed Hand A has natural-language inflection (mean 2.46 distinct suffixes per frequent skeleton) and LDA topic-section purity 0.831 — compatible with a recipe corpus where instruction verbs inflect and topics cluster by section

Four consecutive refutations of per-plant content decoding (`H-BV-SYLLABLE-LEVEL-01`, `H-BV-HAND-A-CONTENT-INDEPENDENCE-01`, `H-BV-CONSONANT-ID-01`, `H-BV-CONSONANT-SEMANTICS-01`) all consistent with 'Hand A is generic-instructional text, not plant-specific description' — i.e. recipes with reusable verbs
Medieval pharmaceutical recipe manuals (e.g. Circa Instans, Macer Floridus, Antidotarium Nicolai) have this exact structure: ingredient header, then preparation, then dosage
evidence_against: Paragraph boundaries inferred from line-initial plain gallows may be noisy; only 271 paragraphs across 11,022 tokens means mean paragraph length ~40 tokens, which is long for a recipe
If each recipe has a unique ingredient, paragraph-initial tokens might NOT repeat much — Test 4's 10% recurrence threshold may be optimistic
The recipe-verb prediction (top medial tokens = inflected verbs) competes with the null that top medial tokens are just FREQUENT words for any reason (conjunctions, articles, anaphora)
test:
LOCKED FOUR-CRITERION PROCEDURE on Hand A.

  Paragraphs: 271 detected in H-BV-HIGH-LOW-STRUCTURE-01 via line-initial plain gallows t/p markers. Same detection method here.
  HIGH_TYPES = 146 types from H-BV-NOMENCLATOR-01 (cumulative 50.1%).
  LOW_TYPES  = 3,367 types.

  POSITION BINS per paragraph (length N tokens):
    INITIAL    = position 0
    FIRST      = positions 1 .. ceil(N/3) - 1
    MIDDLE     = positions ceil(N/3) .. ceil(2N/3) - 1
    FINAL      = positions ceil(2N/3) .. N - 1

  CRITERION 1 — VOCABULARY DISJUNCTION (Test 2 in user spec):
    Aggregate types appearing at paragraph-INITIAL position (I) and types
    appearing anywhere in MIDDLE position (M) across all paragraphs.
    Disjunction = |I \ M| / |I| (fraction of INITIAL types that never
    appear medially).
    PASS if disjunction >= 0.70.

  CRITERION 2 — RECIPE-VERB PROPERTIES (Test 3):
    Top-20 most frequent MIDDLE-position tokens. For each:
      (a) inflection: number of distinct types that strip to the same
          consonant skeleton (via Brady's EVA->skeleton map used in prior
          Brain-V tests). Inflected if >=2 distinct variants.
      (b) cross-folio: the token appears on >=30% of Hand-A folios.
    Token passes if BOTH (a) and (b). Criterion passes if >=15 of 20
    top medial tokens pass.

  CRITERION 3 — LOW-CLASS INITIAL RECURRENCE (Test 4):
    Among the 237 LOW-class paragraph-INITIAL tokens, count types that
    initiate >=3 distinct paragraphs.
    Let n_recur be that count; n_low_init_types = total distinct LOW
    types appearing at paragraph-INITIAL.
    PASS if n_recur / n_low_init_types >= 0.10.
test_threshold: CONFIRMED (pharmaceutical recipes) if all 3 criteria pass. MARGINAL if exactly 2 pass — follow up with ingredient-clustering test on recurring LOW-class initials. REFUTED (not recipe structure) if 0 or 1 of 3 pass.

tests_run.details: MARGINAL per locked rule (2 of 3 criteria pass). CRITERION 1 PASS: disjunction ratio 0.850 (threshold >=0.70) — 176 of 207 paragraph-INITIAL types never appear in paragraph-medial position. Paragraph openers use a vocabulary almost entirely separate from running-text vocabulary. CRITERION 2 PASS: 19 of 20 top-MIDDLE tokens show recipe-verb properties (>=2 skeleton variants AND >=30% Hand-A folio coverage). The top-medial tokens — daiin (147 occ, 93% folios, 15 variants), chol (104, 81.6%, 26 variants), s (56, 66.7%, 32 variants), chor (55, 71.9%, 33 variants), sho, y, ol, shol, dy, chey, cheol, chy, cthy, or, dain, dal, aiin, dar, shy — collectively look like a generic-instructional vocabulary reused across topics. CRITERION 3 FAIL: only 9 of 197 distinct LOW-class paragraph-initial types (4.6%) initiate >=3 paragraphs, below the 10% threshold. Paragraph headers are NEARLY ALL UNIQUE: the nine recurring heads are tor, pchor, tshol, pcheol, tcheody, teol, tcho, tshor, pchocthy. Combined reading: paragraphs are topically headed + instructional-body, but HEADERS DO NOT REPEAT the way a classical pharmaceutical ingredient list would. This is more consistent with a HERBAL-ENCYCLOPEDIC structure (one unique topic or plant per paragraph, generic descriptive verbs across all) than a medicinal recipe manual where ingredients recur.

verdict_note: 2 of 3 criteria pass; locked rule yields MARGINAL. The disjunction (C1) and recipe-verb (C2) signals are STRONG — paragraph vocabulary is clearly stratified into unique headers + reusable body tokens. But paragraph-initial types do NOT recur across paragraphs (C3 FAIL at 4.6% vs 10% threshold), which argues AGAINST a classical pharmaceutical recipe manual where ingredients repeat. The alternative that fits all three observations (unique headers + reusable body vocabulary + no header recurrence) is a HERBAL-ENCYCLOPEDIC entry-per-paragraph structure — one entry per plant or topic, with descriptive/property vocabulary (Galenic qualities, uses, preparation verbs) shared across all entries. Medieval encyclopedic herbals like Circa Instans and Macer Floridus have exactly this structure.
findings_for_followup: Ingredient-clustering follow-up: do paragraph-initial tokens on plant-ID folios cluster by plant family or species? If paragraph headers on folios of similar plants have higher pairwise similarity than random, the header vocabulary is plant-taxonomic and the structure is herbal-encyclopedic.

Compare paragraph-header recurrence on Hand A against an 11k-token excerpt of Circa Instans or Macer Floridus (if digitised): if natural herbal encyclopedia paragraphs also have ~5% header recurrence, Criterion 3's threshold was too strict for this structure.

Test whether paragraph openers correlate with folio illustration content — do paragraph-initial tokens appear on folios illustrating plants with similar visual features? This would link the header vocabulary to botanical identity.

The top-2 medial tokens are daiin and chol, which Pagel (2025) translated as 'is given (datur)' and 'leaf (folium)'. Regardless of Pagel's readings, their statistical profile here (high cross-folio reuse, high morphological variance) is exactly what a medieval Latin herbal verb (`datur`) and a recurring anatomical noun (`folium`) would look like. Worth separate pre-registration with Pagel's readings as the locked prediction.
reasoning: The combined findings of NOMENCLATOR-01, NLREF-01, HIGH-DECODE-01, and HIGH-LOW-STRUCTURE-01 consistently point at a two-register prose with topic-initial rare tokens and instruction-medial common tokens. Medieval pharmaceutical recipes are the most parsimonious generative model for this signature. This test operationalises three independent predictions of the recipe model and scores them against locked thresholds.
source: Brain-V 2026-04-16 user-directed test after HIGH-LOW-STRUCTURE-01 surfaced regime-alternation and topic-initial LOW signals
